normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.requirement_title: The app prevents identification of the user.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-PRIVACY-2 (Description): Protecting user identity is crucial. This control emphasizes the use of unlinkability techniques like data abstraction, anonymization and pseudonymization to prevent user identification and tracking. Another key aspect addressed by this control is to establish technical barriers when employing complex 'fingerprint-like' signals (e.g. device IDs, IP addresses, behavioral patterns) for specific purposes. For instance, a fingerprint used for fraud detection should be isolated and not repurposed for audience measurement in an analytics SDK. This ensures that each data stream serves its intended function without risking user privacy.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must prevent user identification and unauthorized tracking through three substantive disciplines: use unlinkability techniques (data abstraction, anonymization, pseudonymization) wherever possible to prevent direct user identification; restrict use of persistent device identifiers (IDFA on iOS, AAID on Android) and randomize per-session identifiers; and establish purpose-scoped isolation of fingerprint-like signals — a fingerprint legitimately used for fraud detection must not be repurposed for analytics or audience measurement. No cross-app or cross-device tracking by default; any tracking requires explicit consent.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies at Tier 2+ as a conservative corpus-level gate. Per Constitution Pillar 2 (Data & Identity Discipline), user-identification minimization activates at Tier 2+ where the app handles content distribution and analytics; the dedicated trace SEC-PILLAR-2-USER-IDENTIFICATION-MINIMIZATION is a Tier 2-5 control. The blueprint schema does not yet declare a discrete identifier-strategy declaration field as a top-level applicability filter, so security_manifest.app_tier >= 2 is used as a broad proxy. If a Tier 2+ app explicitly declares no identifier-collection posture (e.g., a fully-anonymous read-only utility with no analytics) and supplies justification, auditors may mark the requirement not-applicable during evidence review with documented justification in security_manifest. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 2+ requires user-identification minimization because content-distribution and analytics-bearing apps inherently risk identifier accumulation that must be deliberately restricted.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 2's SEC-PILLAR-2-USER-IDENTIFICATION-MINIMIZATION trace and MASVS-PRIVACY-2 describe the same control by name and substance. The Constitution trace requires restriction of persistent device identifiers (IDFA on iOS, AAID on Android), avoidance of fingerprinting techniques (canvas, audio, font, sensor-based identification), randomization of per-session identifiers where possible, no cross-app or cross-device tracking by default, and explicit consent declared in the manifest for any tracking. This directly implements MASVS-PRIVACY-2's requirement to prevent user identification through unlinkability, identifier discipline, and purpose-scoped isolation of fingerprint-like signals. The mapping is direct because the trace and the MASVS control state the same requirement at the same level of abstraction. Implementation correctness for purpose-scoped isolation (the explicit anti-pattern PRIVACY-2 calls out — fraud-detection fingerprints not repurposed for analytics), AppTrackingTransparency status enforcement on iOS, Advertising ID opt-out respect on Android, and pseudonymization technique selection requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. The mapping is direct at the control-statement level, but the auditor evaluation chain (file_standards_auditor + reaper-privacy + reaper-secrets) is necessary for full evaluation of correctness.

expected_evidence_patterns: iOS: AppTrackingTransparency framework integration — ATTrackingManager.requestTrackingAuthorization() called before any cross-app tracking; IDFA only accessed when status == .authorized
iOS: Info.plist declares NSUserTrackingUsageDescription with user-meaningful justification string
iOS: PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy declares NSPrivacyTracking and NSPrivacyTrackingDomains accurately reflecting actual cross-app/cross-site tracking surface
Android: AdvertisingIdClient.getAdvertisingIdInfo() consulted with respect for isLimitAdTrackingEnabled() and LAT (Limit Ad Tracking) signal; no fallback to AAID when user has opted out
Android: data safety section declares whether app collects 'User or device IDs' and the purpose; ID collection scoped to declared purpose only
Per-session identifier generation uses cryptographically random sources (Apple's Security.framework SecRandomCopyBytes, Android's SecureRandom) rather than UUID-based device-stable patterns

Fingerprint-like signals (device characteristics aggregated for fraud detection) isolated by purpose — separate code paths for fraud-detection-fingerprint vs analytics-identifier with no shared identifier store; documented in security_manifest

Pseudonymization applied where direct identifiers would otherwise be stored or transmitted: scoped pseudonymous IDs (derived per purpose from secure inputs) used for analytics references; salt/pepper-managed hashes for matching-but-not-revealing contexts, with salts and peppers stored in secure storage (Keychain on iOS, Keystore-backed EncryptedSharedPreferences on Android) and rotated per documented policy — pseudonymization reduces linkability, it does not fully anonymize
failure_indicators: iOS: IDFA accessed without prior ATTrackingManager.requestTrackingAuthorization() call — Apple App Store rejection risk plus PRIVACY-2 violation
iOS: IDFA accessed and used after status == .denied or .notDetermined — disrespecting user's explicit choice or default privacy posture
Android: AdvertisingIdClient.getAdvertisingIdInfo() consulted but isLimitAdTrackingEnabled() ignored — collecting AAID despite user opt-out signal

Fingerprinting techniques (canvas fingerprint via WebView, audio context fingerprint, font enumeration, sensor-output fingerprint) implemented without explicit consent and purpose justification declared in security_manifest
Same fingerprint or device-stable identifier passed to multiple purpose contexts — fraud-detection fingerprint forwarded to analytics SDK (the explicit anti-pattern PRIVACY-2 calls out)
Per-session identifiers that are actually persistent — generated once and cached to disk rather than regenerated per session — defeats the unlinkability intent
Cross-app or cross-device tracking enabled by default — should require explicit consent and declared tracking purpose

Direct identifiers (email, phone number) used as analytics reference paths without pseudonymization layer — should be scoped pseudonymous IDs or salt/pepper-managed hashes with secure salt/pepper storage; raw direct identifiers in analytics paths increase linkability surface unnecessarily
Apple PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy declares NSPrivacyTracking false while code actually performs cross-app tracking — misalignment between declared and actual
false_positive_traps: Identifier handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test fixtures legitimately use stable test-IDs; these are not production violations.

Login-required apps that store the user's account identifier (email, username, account ID) for authentication purposes are NOT violations — the account identifier is the user's known identity, not anonymous-tracking surface; the violation surface is identifier accumulation BEYOND the authentication scope.

Fraud-detection fingerprints used WITHIN a strict purpose-scoped boundary (separate signing key, separate storage, no cross-purpose plumbing) are NOT violations even though they exist — the violation surface is repurposing across boundaries.

Per-app-install random identifiers (Apple's identifierForVendor, Android's installation UUID generated at first launch) are NOT violations as long as they aren't combined with device-stable signals or shared with third parties beyond declared scope — they're the recommended alternative to IDFA/AAID for app-instance-scoped identification.

Server-side persistent user identifiers (account UUIDs, customer IDs) tied to authenticated sessions are NOT MASVS-PRIVACY-2 violations — PRIVACY-2 targets unauthorized identification and cross-app tracking, not authenticated account identity within the app's own backend.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 2 user-identification-minimization discipline (SEC-PILLAR-2-USER-IDENTIFICATION-MINIMIZATION) as primary evidence toward MASVS-PRIVACY-2 coverage
restricts persistent device identifier access (IDFA via AppTrackingTransparency on iOS, AAID via opt-out-respect on Android) per MASVS-PRIVACY-2 platform-best-practice requirement
isolates fingerprint-like signals by purpose — fraud-detection fingerprints not repurposed for analytics or audience measurement per MASVS-PRIVACY-2 explicit anti-pattern

randomizes per-session identifiers and applies pseudonymization (scoped pseudonymous IDs or salt/pepper-managed hashes with secure salt/pepper storage) for analytics-reference paths — reduces linkability, does not claim full anonymization
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-PRIVACY-2. MASVS-PRIVACY-2 is mapped to one Constitution v0.5 trace in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-2-USER-IDENTIFICATION-MINIMIZATION (primary, direct). The mapping is direct because the trace and the MASVS control state the same requirement at the same level of abstraction. Claims about user-identification prevention depend on the chosen techniques — claims about IDFA/AAID restriction require ATTrackingManager / AdvertisingIdClient opt-out respect; claims about fingerprint isolation require documented purpose-scoped code-path separation; claims about pseudonymization are scoped to linkability reduction (scoped pseudonymous IDs, salt/pepper-managed hashes with secure salt/pepper management) and do NOT extend to full-anonymization assertions — pseudonymization reduces linkability but does not de-identify, and the salt/pepper management approach (secure storage, rotation cadence) determines the strength of the reduction. Out of scope: server-side authenticated-user identity within the app's own backend (PRIVACY-2 targets unauthorized identification and cross-app tracking, not legitimate authenticated-session identity), full-anonymization claims under k-anonymity / differential-privacy frameworks (separate technical and audit requirements), and privacy assessments under GDPR / CCPA (MASVS-PRIVACY disclaims substitution for these per the standard's IMPORTANT DISCLAIMER on page 33). Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
